_about.name: comp-metrics-by-industry
_about.purpose: One-time sector reasoning for the one-pager comp table (Valuation vs Peers). The engine LOOKS UP the metric set instead of re-deriving it per idea, so two miner one-pagers a month apart show the same columns.
_about.taxonomy_source: FMP /stable/available-industries (159 industries) and /stable/available-sectors (11 sectors), pulled 2026-07-09. Every industry string is mapped explicitly.
_about.scope: v1 covers comp-table columns only. The same groups can later drive the page-1 stat strip and full-report appendix tables.
how_to_resolve: 1. Look up the subject company's FMP profile.industry in industry_to_group (exact string match).
2. If the industry is missing or empty, fall back to sector_fallback[profile.sector].
3. If neither resolves, use default_group, and log the unmapped industry string so the file can be extended.
4. Peers inherit the SUBJECT's group so every row shows the same columns.
universal_rules: Rows: company name plus ticker ('Caledonia Mining (CMCL.L)'); subject row bold with its actual name, never '(this name)'. Truncate long names, never tickers.
Computed metrics: identical formula for subject and peers, from the same data snapshot, priced at the same day's close. Never mix price epochs on one page.
Filing facts: from company releases, guidance preferred and labeled; print 'n/a' when not found. Never guess, never backfill from a different period silently.
sourced_only metrics: include the column only when a real source exists for every row that shows a value.
Stage tags: every peer gets a short stage/product tag (producer, ramp-up, developer, gold+copper...). Earnings multiples of ramp-ups and developers print 'n/m'.
Prose anchors: when the memo cites a peer multiple range for re-rating, use only peers that pass the group's clean_comp_rule.
Aim for 4-6 columns; if the layout gets tight, remove columns in drop_first_if_tight order.
Currency hygiene: label currency per row if peers report in different currencies; on UK names, GBp is pence and GBP is pounds -- market caps are GBP, prices are usually GBp.
_about.example_lookup: Thor Explorations: profile.industry='Gold' -> miners_precious -> columns: Prod (koz), AISC ($/oz), P/E, EV/EBITDA, FCF Yield, Div Yield; peers tagged producer/ramp-up/gold+copper; re-rating range from producing gold comps only.

ev_ebitda.label: EV/EBITDA
ev_ebitda.rule: n/m if EBITDA <= 0; never show for banks or insurance carriers

p_tbv.label: P/TBV
p_tbv.how: market cap / (common equity - goodwill - other intangibles)
p_tbv.rule: print 'neg' if tangible equity <= 0; P/TBV can never be below P/B

p_s.label: P/S
p_s.rule: n/m if revenue is de-minimis (pre-revenue and ramp-up names)

ev_s.label: EV/S
ev_s.rule: always pair with rev_growth_3y; one without the other misleads

fcf_yield.label: FCF Yield
fcf_yield.how: (operating cash flow - capex, TTM) / market cap
fcf_yield.rule: sanity: FCF above EBITDA is almost always a data error

div_yield.label: Div Yield
div_yield.how: indicated regular annual DPS / price at the day's close
div_yield.rule: specials and one-off bonuses shown separately or footnoted, never blended silently; if only TTM total is available, label it 'TTM incl. specials'

rev_growth_3y.label: Rev Growth (3Y)
rev_growth_3y.how: 3-year revenue CAGR (YoY if under 3 years of history, labeled)

rote.label: RoTE
rote.how: net income (TTM) / average tangible common equity

nd_ebitda.label: Net Debt/EBITDA
nd_ebitda.how: (total debt - cash) / EBITDA (TTM); include leases where reported

net_cash.label: Net Cash
net_cash.how: cash + liquid investments - total debt, absolute currency

p_ffo.label: P/FFO
p_ffo.how: price / FFO per share; use company-reported FFO when available, else approximate NI + D&A - gains on property sales and label 'approx'

ltv.label: LTV
ltv.how: net debt / gross real estate assets (approximation of loan-to-value)

prod_koz.label: Prod (koz)
prod_koz.how: gold/PM production in koz; current-year guidance preferred, else latest FY actual, labeled

aisc_oz.label: AISC ($/oz)
aisc_oz.how: all-in sustaining cost per ounce; guidance preferred, labeled

prod_units.label: Production
prod_units.how: production in the commodity's native units (kt Cu, Mlbs U3O8, Mt coal...)

unit_cash_cost.label: Cash Cost
unit_cash_cost.how: cash cost per native production unit

prod_boed.label: Prod (boe/d)
prod_boed.how: oil & gas production, boe/d, with liquids share if disclosed

netback_boe.label: Netback ($/boe)
netback_boe.how: operating netback per boe from company reporting

cet1.label: CET1
cet1.how: common equity tier 1 ratio from regulatory reporting

combined_ratio.label: Combined Ratio
combined_ratio.how: P&C/reinsurance combined ratio from company reporting
combined_ratio.rule: n/a for life insurers and where not disclosed

backlog.label: Backlog
backlog.how: order backlog or book-to-bill from company reporting
backlog.rule: optional column; n/a if not disclosed

aum.label: AUM / Flows
aum.how: assets under management and latest net flows

runway_quarters.label: Cash Runway
runway_quarters.how: quarters of cash at current operating burn, from company statements if given, else computed from net cash / quarterly burn and labeled 'approx'

p_nav.label: P/NAV
p_nav.how: price / net asset value ONLY from a company-stated or published consensus NAV
p_nav.rule: omit the column entirely if no source; never estimate NAV

ev_resource_oz.label: EV/oz
ev_resource_oz.how: enterprise value / total resource ounces from the company's compliant resource statement (43-101/JORC)
ev_resource_oz.rule: omit if no current resource statement

miners_precious.label: Precious metals miners
miners_precious.stage_rule: Tag each row producer / ramp-up / developer. Ramp-up = first production within the last 4 quarters or revenue still immaterial; developer = no production. Print n/m for earnings multiples of ramp-ups and developers; for developers the relevant line is EV vs net cash and project NPV (sourced).
miners_precious.clean_comp_rule: Clean anchors share primary metal AND producing stage. A gold+copper transition name or a ramp-up does not anchor a re-rating range.
miners_precious.pitfalls: P/B is historical capex and impairments, P/S ignores the cost curve entirely; both carry almost no information here. A developer in a producer table shows meaningless P/S (e.g. 48.6x) and dashes.

miners_base_bulk.label: Base metals, uranium, coal and bulk miners
miners_base_bulk.stage_rule: Same producer / ramp-up / developer tagging as precious metals; n/m multiples for non-producers.
miners_base_bulk.clean_comp_rule: Match primary commodity first; a copper name is not a coal comp even if both sit in one screen bucket.
miners_base_bulk.pitfalls: Cyclical earnings make spot P/E unreliable at both extremes; state where in the price cycle the multiple is measured.

steel.label: Steel producers
steel.clean_comp_rule: Match route (EAF vs blast furnace) and region where possible; note import exposure.
steel.pitfalls: Deep cyclical: trough P/E looks expensive and peak P/E looks cheap; P/TBV and mid-cycle EV/EBITDA carry the signal.

materials_processing.label: Chemicals, paper, packaging, construction materials and other processed materials
materials_processing.clean_comp_rule: Match sub-vertical (specialty vs commodity chemistry, containerboard vs printing paper); commodity and specialty names deserve different multiples.
materials_processing.pitfalls: Commodity-price pass-through distorts single-year margins; prefer mid-cycle framing in prose.

agri_commodity.label: Farm products, agricultural commodities and milling
agri_commodity.clean_comp_rule: Match crop/protein exposure and degree of vertical integration.
agri_commodity.pitfalls: Thin, volatile commodity margins; balance sheet (land, biological assets) often matters more than the income statement.

oil_gas_ep.label: Oil & gas exploration and production
oil_gas_ep.stage_rule: Tag producer / development-stage. Pre-production names get n/m multiples; the story is EV vs 2P reserves and funding.
oil_gas_ep.clean_comp_rule: Match liquids vs gas weighting and basin/jurisdiction; a gassy producer is not an oily comp.
oil_gas_ep.pitfalls: P/E distorted by DD&A, impairments and hedge accounting. Reserves (2P, PV-10) belong in the appendix, not guessed into the table.

oil_gas_integrated.label: Integrated oil & gas
oil_gas_integrated.clean_comp_rule: Compare integrateds with integrateds; upstream pure-plays distort the range.
oil_gas_integrated.pitfalls: Downstream/chemicals segments mask upstream economics; note segment mix when the gap is large.

oil_gas_midstream.label: Midstream / pipelines
oil_gas_midstream.clean_comp_rule: Match contract profile (fee-based vs commodity-exposed) and leverage.
oil_gas_midstream.pitfalls: Distribution coverage and contracted cash flows matter more than P/E; if distributable cash flow is reported, cite it from filings.

oil_gas_refining.label: Refining & marketing
oil_gas_refining.clean_comp_rule: Match refining complexity and region; marketing-heavy names are different animals.
oil_gas_refining.pitfalls: Crack-spread cyclicality: single-year multiples mislead in both directions; say where spreads are vs history.

oil_gas_services.label: Oilfield services & drilling
oil_gas_services.clean_comp_rule: Match service line and fleet type (onshore vs offshore); day-rate cycles differ.
oil_gas_services.pitfalls: Asset values swing with activity; P/TBV plus backlog beats earnings multiples at cycle turns.

banks.label: Banks
banks.clean_comp_rule: Match geography and model (retail/commercial vs universal/IB-heavy); cross-border P/TBV gaps often reflect structural RoTE and rates, name that in prose.
banks.pitfalls: EV, EBITDA, P/S and FCF yield are meaningless for banks; never show them. P/TBV must be read against RoTE: cheap on book with single-digit RoTE may be fairly priced.

insurance_carriers.label: Insurance carriers (life, P&C, specialty, reinsurance)
insurance_carriers.clean_comp_rule: Match line of business: life vs P&C vs reinsurance are not comps for each other; combined ratio applies to P&C/re only.
insurance_carriers.pitfalls: EV/EBITDA meaningless; book value quality (reserve adequacy) is the real question and belongs in prose.

asset_managers.label: Asset managers
asset_managers.clean_comp_rule: Match asset class mix and fee model (traditional vs alternatives vs passive).
asset_managers.pitfalls: Watch the classification: on non-US exchanges FMP often labels closed-end funds and investment trusts 'Asset Management'. A fund is not an operating company; compare it on discount to NAV or exclude it from the idea universe.

exchanges_data.label: Exchanges and financial data
exchanges_data.clean_comp_rule: Match revenue mix (transaction vs recurring data/index fees).
exchanges_data.pitfalls: Structurally high multiples; a 'cheap' exchange usually has a volume or regulatory story, name it.

capital_markets_ib.label: Investment banks and capital-markets firms
capital_markets_ib.pitfalls: Earnings are cyclical and comp-driven; tangible book plus through-cycle RoTE is the anchor.

specialty_finance_credit.label: Consumer credit, specialty finance and mortgage finance
specialty_finance_credit.clean_comp_rule: Match asset class (cards vs auto vs mortgage) and funding model (deposit vs wholesale).
specialty_finance_credit.pitfalls: Credit costs lag the cycle; cheap P/E late-cycle is a warning, not a bargain. Charge-off and provisioning trends belong in prose from filings.

financial_other.label: Diversified financials, financial conglomerates, shells
financial_other.clean_comp_rule: Usually SOTP situations; comp each segment against its own peer set rather than the conglomerate against anything.
financial_other.pitfalls: Shell companies (SPACs) should normally be excluded from the idea universe, not comped.

reits_equity.label: Equity REITs (all property types)
reits_equity.clean_comp_rule: Match property type first (industrial vs office vs retail vs healthcare); cap-rate environments differ by type.
reits_equity.pitfalls: P/E is meaningless for REITs because depreciation swamps it; FFO is the earnings line. P/TBV only proxies NAV where assets are carried near market (IFRS names), label accordingly.

reits_mortgage.label: Mortgage REITs
reits_mortgage.clean_comp_rule: Match book (agency vs credit) and leverage.
reits_mortgage.pitfalls: Book value marks move with rates; trailing yield says little about forward earnings power.

real_estate_ops.label: Real estate operators and developers (non-REIT)
real_estate_ops.clean_comp_rule: Separate rent-collectors from developers; development earnings are lumpy and deserve lower multiples.
real_estate_ops.pitfalls: Carrying values vary by accounting regime (historical cost vs fair value); say which basis P/TBV is on.

software.label: Software, internet content, gaming
software.clean_comp_rule: Match growth band and model (subscription vs license vs ad-funded); EV/S without the growth column is banned.
software.pitfalls: Unprofitable names show n/m P/E; that is information, not a gap. Stock comp can make FCF flatter than economics.

semis.label: Semiconductors and semi equipment
semis.clean_comp_rule: Match position (fabless vs IDM vs equipment) and end-market exposure.
semis.pitfalls: Deep cyclicality: state where the cycle sits when quoting the multiple; trough earnings inflate P/E exactly when the stock is cheapest.

tech_hardware.label: Hardware, electronics, communication equipment
tech_hardware.clean_comp_rule: Match product cycle exposure and customer concentration.
tech_hardware.pitfalls: Inventory cycles distort single quarters; check working-capital swings behind FCF.

telecom.label: Telecom operators
telecom.clean_comp_rule: Match infrastructure ownership (network owner vs MVNO) and geography.
telecom.pitfalls: Capex intensity differs by build cycle; EBITDA alone overstates comparability, FCF yield corrects it.

media.label: Media, broadcasting, publishing, entertainment
media.clean_comp_rule: Match revenue model (advertising vs subscription vs content licensing); ad-cyclicals and subscription names are different comps.
media.pitfalls: Structural decline in legacy formats can make optically cheap multiples a value trap; the prose must say which side of that line the name is on.

pharma.label: Pharmaceutical manufacturers
pharma.clean_comp_rule: Match scale and patent-cliff exposure; generics and originators are separate comp sets.
pharma.pitfalls: Pipeline value and patent expiries live outside the multiples; flag the next major expiry in prose from filings.

biotech.label: Biotechnology (mostly pre-profit)
biotech.stage_rule: Pre-revenue: all earnings multiples n/m; the table is EV vs net cash and runway. Commercial-stage: add ev_s and growth.
biotech.clean_comp_rule: Comp by stage and modality only with real caution; most biotech 'comps' are decorative.
biotech.pitfalls: A biotech trading below net cash is a real screen hit; everything else about the valuation lives in the science, which no multiple captures.

medtech_healthcare.label: Medical devices, instruments, diagnostics, specialties
medtech_healthcare.clean_comp_rule: Match regulatory moat and recurring-revenue share (consumables vs capital equipment).
medtech_healthcare.pitfalls: Capital-equipment names are cyclical against hospital budgets; consumables deserve the premium.

healthcare_providers_plans.label: Care facilities and healthcare plans
healthcare_providers_plans.clean_comp_rule: Plans comp with plans (medical-loss ratio from filings), facilities with facilities (lease-adjusted leverage).
healthcare_providers_plans.pitfalls: Reimbursement policy risk dominates; leases make facility leverage look lighter than it is.

retail_distribution.label: Retail and distribution (all verticals)
retail_distribution.clean_comp_rule: Match vertical and format (grocer with grocer, distributor with distributor); online/offline mix noted.
retail_distribution.pitfalls: Leases: use lease-including leverage or say you have not. Same-store sales from filings beat any multiple for direction. Thin-margin distributors: P/S is nearly useless, working capital is the business.

consumer_brands.label: Consumer brands (food, beverage, household, apparel brands, luxury, tobacco)
consumer_brands.clean_comp_rule: Match category and brand strength; private-label-exposed names are not brand comps.
consumer_brands.pitfalls: Volume vs price mix behind revenue growth matters; a 'cheap' brand losing volume is melting.

restaurants_leisure.label: Restaurants, leisure, gaming, lodging, travel
restaurants_leisure.clean_comp_rule: Match asset model (franchised vs owned; asset-light vs property-heavy casinos/hotels).
restaurants_leisure.pitfalls: Leases and whole-business EBITDA hide obligations; franchise systems and owner-operators deserve different multiples.

autos.label: Auto manufacturers, parts, recreational vehicles
autos.clean_comp_rule: OEMs with OEMs, suppliers with suppliers; premium vs volume OEMs noted.
autos.pitfalls: Captive finance arms distort EV and leverage; use industrial net cash/debt ex-financial-services where disclosed (filing fact), and say so.

homebuilders.label: Homebuilders and residential construction
homebuilders.clean_comp_rule: Match geography and price point; land-bank strategy (owned vs optioned) changes book quality.
homebuilders.pitfalls: Book value is land and inventory: the cycle question is whether it is carried below or above market. Peak-cycle P/E always looks cheap.

construction_engineering.label: E&C, construction, infrastructure operations
construction_engineering.clean_comp_rule: Match contract type (fixed-price vs cost-plus) and end market.
construction_engineering.pitfalls: Percentage-of-completion accounting hides problem contracts; backlog quality beats backlog size.

industrial_manufacturing.label: Machinery, capital goods, electrical equipment, A&D, conglomerates, rentals
industrial_manufacturing.clean_comp_rule: Match end market and cycle position; defense primes carry backlog (filing) as an optional column.
industrial_manufacturing.pitfalls: Depreciation policies vary; EV/EBIT is the harder test than EV/EBITDA for asset-heavy names (and for equipment-rental fleets).

transport.label: Airlines, rail, trucking, freight & logistics
transport.clean_comp_rule: Mode with mode: rail vs trucking vs airline economics are not comparable; airlines need lease-including EV.
transport.pitfalls: Fuel and rate cycles drive single-year numbers; operating ratio (filings) is the industry's own language for rail/trucking.

shipping.label: Marine shipping
shipping.clean_comp_rule: Match vessel class (tanker vs dry bulk vs container vs LNG) and charter coverage (spot vs time-chartered).
shipping.pitfalls: The real book is fleet market value: P/TBV against stated book differs from P/NAV against broker valuations; charter-rate cyclicality makes trailing multiples nearly meaningless at turns.

waste_env.label: Waste and environmental services
waste_env.clean_comp_rule: Match route density/contract structure; hazardous vs solid waste differ.
waste_env.pitfalls: Landfill capex cycles depress FCF temporarily; distinguish growth from maintenance capex where disclosed.

asset_light_services.label: Asset-light services (consulting, staffing, IT services, brokers, agencies, education)
asset_light_services.clean_comp_rule: Match service line and client cyclicality (staffing is early-cycle, consulting mid, audit stable).
asset_light_services.pitfalls: People walk out of the door; margin durability is culture, which no multiple shows. Working capital is the balance-sheet tell.

utilities.label: Utilities (regulated, diversified, IPPs, renewables)
utilities.clean_comp_rule: Regulated with regulated (compare allowed-return regimes), merchant/IPP with merchant; renewables developers are a third bucket.
utilities.pitfalls: Leverage is structural, not distress; the question is regulatory regime and rate-base growth (filings), not the raw multiple.

general.label: Default when no industry mapping applies
general.clean_comp_rule: Same business model and size class; when in doubt, fewer but cleaner comps.
general.pitfalls: If a name lands here, consider whether the industry mapping needs a new entry; log it rather than silently defaulting.
